summary: Gruber revisits a 2012 disagreement with audio engineer Zach Phillips about whether iMessage on Mac should use phone numbers as identifiers. Gruber had argued phone number iMessages should stay on iPhone only, while Phillips argued iMessages should drop email addresses entirely. Both were wrong because they missed the deeper insight: messages are between people, not between phone numbers or devices. Gruber uses this as a springboard to praise Apple's iMessage strategy, arguing that what appeared to be free iPhone-to-iPhone SMS was really the bootstrapping of an entirely new worldwide messaging platform.
key_points: Gruber admits he was wrong in 2012 to insist iMessage phone numbers should be iPhone-only, stuck in a mental model separating SMS replacement from instant messaging
Phillips was also partially wrong for arguing iMessages should never use email addresses — the real insight is that identifiers serve people, not devices or protocols
Messages are between people, and phone numbers and email addresses are just addressing mechanisms — a point both commentators missed at the time
iMessage's takeover of SMS was a deliberate strategy to bootstrap a new secure worldwide messaging platform, not merely a free texting feature
Apple's press briefing practices have changed — the kind of behind-the-scenes reporting Gruber did for Mountain Lion's 2012 announcement is no longer permitted
money_quotes: What we both missed is that messages aren't between phone numbers or email addresses or specific devices — they're between people.
Phone numbers and email addresses are just identifiers used to address those people.

What we initially saw as 'free SMS for iPhone-to-iPhone text messages' was really the bootstrapping of an altogether new (and secure) worldwide messaging platform — a platform that today is an immeasurably valuable asset for Apple.

Just about everyone today acknowledges that iMessage's usurpation of text messaging from the carrier-controlled SMS was a stroke of genius on Apple's part, but I think few appreciate just how deft their strategy and execution were.
I'll score this as me being very wrong, and Phillips being a little wrong.
It's like agreeing to write about a movie, but not the experience of the movie theater in which you saw it.
key_insight: iMessage's true genius wasn't free texting — it was reframing messaging as communication between people rather than between phone numbers or devices, bootstrapping an invaluable platform in the process.
